title: Childhood Cancer Clinical Data Commons: A New Web Application for Your Data Needs
body:
If you use childhood cancer data in your research, explore the Childhood Cancer Clinical Data Commons (C3DC): a new, open-access, web application from NCI’s Childhood Cancer Data Initiative (CCDI).

Using C3DC, you can:

  • search for harmonized, deidentified, participant-level clinical data from multiple studies.
  • create custom cohorts.
  • download data for local analysis.

You’ll have access to the following resources:

  • C3DC Data Model: Explore a representation of harmonized data from CCDI and other studies.
  • GitHub Harmonization Repository: Find harmonization scripts, translation files, and more.
  • CCDI Hub: Get information about available tools and applications. You’ll also find descriptions of resources for specific areas of cancer research.
  • Genomic Data Commons Data Portal: Search and download cancer data for your analysis.
  • Cancer Data Standards Repository: Find over 60,000 common data elements.

Check back regularly as you will find new pediatric cancer data and functionality added to the data commons over time.

Visit the C3DC webpage to explore all the tools available to you. Email CCDI if you have questions about C3DC.

title: Childhood Cancer Data Initiative—Recent Activities and Next Steps
body:
Curious about how things are going with the Childhood Cancer Data Initiative (CCDI) projects?

You can learn about CCDI’s latest progress in a recently published status report.

Read more details about CCDI’s latest efforts in Pediatric Blood & Cancer.

Some of CCDI’s current efforts include:

  • growing the CCDI Data Ecosystem with new resources to improve access to childhood
  • expanding access to molecular characterization to children with high-risk neuroblastoma, and
  • launching the CCDI Coordinated National Initiative for Rare Cancers in Children and Young Adults to put a spotlight on very rare childhood cancers.

Other activities in progress include:

  • developing the CCDI Participant Index, a tool to connect data across the CCDI Data Ecosystem by matching and mapping digital IDs,
  • expanding enrollment and testing types in CCDI’s Molecular Characterization Initiative, and
  • planning a proof-of-concept model to demonstrate the practicality of using technology for obtaining patient consent.

CCDI’s efforts are aimed at helping you and others in the field find new and better ways of using childhood cancer data. The newly published summary offers a window into these latest projects, as well as areas for new research opportunities in the field.

title: Navigating CCDI Hub's Explore Dashboard and Data Access
body:
Learn how to better explore and access Childhood Cancer Data Initiative (CCDI) data in an upcoming webinar!

Peter Gilbert and Sean Burke of the Frederick National Laboratory for Cancer Research will explain how to utilize the “CCDI Hub Explore Dashboard,” a new tool for exploring individual-level files to: investigate data managed by CCDI. establish links between participating patients, samples, and files.

Questions and feedback are welcome to help further enhance the CCDI Hub Explore Dashboard.

Peter Gilbert

Mr. Gilbert is a technical project manager for the Frederick National Laboratory for Cancer Research.

Sean Burke

Mr. Burke is a bioinformatics manager for the Frederick National Laboratory for Cancer Research.

title: Using NCCR*Explorer and NCCR Data in SEER*Stat
body:
You’ll have the opportunity to see live demonstrations of accessing comprehensive cancer-related data during this Childhood Cancer Data Initiative (CCDI) webinar. Dr. Angela Mariotto, chief of the Data Analytics Branch of NCI’s Division of Cancer Control and Population Sciences, will discuss how NCCR*Explorer and SEER*Stat could be used to access, explore, and analyze data that can ultimately lead to improved cancer diagnosis, treatment, and patient care.

By attending the webinar, you’ll learn how to: explore comprehensive cancer-related data such as demographics, diagnosis, treatment, outcomes, etc. conduct data analysis to extract meaningful patterns and trends in surveillance research. use the tools to inform evidence-based decisions in clinical settings, public health policy, or cancer research studies. highlights how to use CCDI’s web applications, platforms, and data, and give attendees the opportunity to learn how to use available resources.

Angela Mariotto, Ph.D.

Dr. Mariotto is the chief of the Data Analytics Branch in the Division of Cancer Control and Population Sciences. Her research interests include development and improvement of cancer progress measures, in particular survival, prevalence, quality of life, utilization, and cost measures.

title: The First Childhood Cancer Data Initiative Community Forum
body:
Join the Childhood Cancer Data Initiative’s (CCDI’s) first Community Forum, bringing together experts, researchers, and community members to discuss CCDI progress and what’s on the horizon for the initiative.

You will hear from multiple NCI staff members and learn about the initiative's plans and activities, including enhancements and additions to CCDI tools and platforms, progress related to the upcoming CCDI Rare Pediatric Cancer Initiative, and more.

Gregory Reaman, Ph.D.

CCDI Scientific Director, NCI

Subhashini Jagu, Ph.D.

Chief, Scientific Policy and Program Branch, NCI

Lynne Penberthy, Ph.D.

Associate Director, Surveillance Research Program, NCI

Jaime Guidry Auvil, Ph.D.

Director, Office of Data Sharing, NCI

Malcolm Smith, Ph.D.

Associate Branch Chief, Pediatrics, NCI

title: Using the Childhood Cancer Data Initiative's Molecular Targets Platform
body:
Attend this next Childhood Cancer Data Initiative (CCDI) webinar to learn about and understand the various use cases of the Molecular Targets Platform (MTP) for your cancer research.

The MTP is a CCDI tool that allows you to navigate, identify, and prioritize potential drug targets in pediatric cancers. It currently includes information sourced from the Food and Drug Administration’s Pediatric Molecular Target Lists and several other projects, but there are plans to add more sources in the future.

Subhashini Jagu, Ph.D.

branch chief and supervisory health scientist administrator.

Mr. Zack Dorman

Mr. Dorman is a technical project manager for NCI’s Center for Technical Operations Support.

title: Using the CCDI Hub and Childhood Cancer Data Catalog
body:
Have you checked out NCI’s Childhood Cancer Data Initiative’s (CCDI’s) new online Hub? It gives you instant access to a rapidly growing inventory of data, tools, and resources on childhood cancer data.

Attend this meeting and learn more about accessing data through the Hub.

You’ll get tips on how to use the Hub to access resources in the CCDI Data Ecosystem. You’ll also learn about the types of data available in the Childhood Cancer Data Catalog and how to access and use these data.

This meeting is the latest in a list of CCDI symposiums, workshops, webinars, and events developed by CCDI to foster collaboration and data sharing within the childhood cancer community of hospitals, clinics, and other stakeholders.

Program Branch A chief, “By empowering the pediatric cancer community with platforms and tools like the CCDI Hub, our goal is to accelerate data-based discovery and encourage collaboration and breakthroughs in biomedical research.”

title: Attend the 2023 NCI Childhood Cancer Data Initiative Annual Symposium
body:
If you’re interested in learning about progress and possibilities in childhood cancer research, register to attend the 2023 NCI Childhood Cancer Data Initiative (CCDI) Annual During the event, you’ll: learn about CCDI’s progress and future opportunities. hear how data collection and sharing can address challenges in childhood cancer research. be able to submit comments and questions.

Virtual Breakout Sessions Want to keep exploring key topics after the symposium is over? Participate in a virtual breakout session! Refer to the symposium webpage for specific times and Webex links.

March 28 for Future Cohort Studies

  • Patient and Family Perspectives on Computable Consent and the CCDI Participant Index March 29
  • Electronic Health Record Data Extraction: Current Status and Continuing Challenges
  • External Controls for Pediatric Cancer Clinical Trials March 30
  • Collaborations and Transformative Research Opportunities Using Data Available Through the CCDI Ecosystem
  • Observational Studies and Novel Interventional Approaches for Rare Pediatric Cancers

title: CCDI Workshop: Advancing a National Initiative for Rare Cancers in Children, Adolescents, and Young Adults
body:
This upcoming Childhood Cancer Data Initiative (CCDI) workshop seeks to speed up progress in the scientific community’s understanding of very rare childhood cancers.

Experts will discuss a range of topics that impact the childhood cancer community’s ability to study and collect important data.

During the workshop, you’ll: explore why collaboration throughout the scientific community is needed to advance progress. learn how CCDI can assist in the process. have the opportunity to ask questions and offer your input. clinical data elements, panel discussions, and other topics that are key to acceleration efforts.

title: CCDI Workshop: The Importance of Electronic Health Record (EHR) Data in Clinical Care and Research
body:
NCI’s Childhood Cancer Data Initiative (CCDI) is hosting a workshop to discuss issues and opportunities for extracting electronic health records (EHR)—health information stored in a digital format—for childhood cancer research.

During the session, the workshop will cover: issues surrounding EHR data portability and interoperability. potential approaches to structuring EHR data for maximal utility and benefit. opportunities to capitalize on the use of EHR data for This workshop is organized by CBIIT Director Dr. Tony Kerlavage and CBIIT Office of Data Sharing Director Dr. Jaime received its first allocation of funding in FY2020, with the goal of learning from every child and young adult with cancer to improve pediatric cancer treatment and data sharing integrity.

If you have any questions, please contact the CCDI team.

title: New Optimized Workflow Offers Comprehensive Characterization of Childhood Cancers
body:
Genomic studies of childhood and rare cancers got a major boost, as a new optimized workflow for managing these data was recently reported in Nature Communications by Dr.

Andrew Kung, senior study author and Chair of the Department of Pediatrics at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center. And, thanks to funding by NCI’s Childhood Cancer Data Initiative (CCDI), it’s easier and faster to share those data with the cancer research community.

Using an end-to-end pipeline for cancer whole genome and transcriptome sequencing (or cWGTS), scientists analyzed samples from 114 pediatric, adolescent, and young adult 9 days, which is a significant improvement over earlier turnaround times of 2–4 weeks. With costs for such testing becoming more manageable, and with this reduced timeframe for generating results, cWGTS offers a promising tool for routine clinical oncology practice for pediatric patients.

The researchers corroborated findings from DNA with evidence from RNA to detect, annotate, and prioritize variants. The integrative assay detected germline, somatic mutation, and fusion genes, which are typically captured using targeted gene-panel assays in adults. However, these tests haven’t been optimized for patients with pediatric or rare cancers, which are driven by distinct molecular features.

The raw whole genome and RNA-sequencing data are available for researchers for further analysis through NCI's General Commons (formerly known as the Cancer Data Service). According to Dr. Subhashini Jagu, a CCDI Data Platform Working Group member, “These data and accompanying analytical workflows have the sensitivity to capture a range of genomic alterations that will help inform the function of genes and post-transcriptional modifications and enable the development of new or more effective therapies for these rare cancers.” Data scientists interested in accessing the data for this study can submit a request to the database of Genotypes and Phenotypes (dbGaP). The data are under controlled-access due to privacy concerns. Requests are managed by NCI’s Data Access Committee and take less than 2 days for approval, with access granted for up to 12 months. Code for reproducing the findings from this article can be found in the GitHub repository. A copy of the processed data is available at the cbioPortal study-specific site. importance of sharing data to empower research into these rare cancers,” said Dr. Kung. “I’m grateful to CCDI for establishing the framework for immediately sharing these large data sets and am proud to also be a part of CCDI’s efforts to transform what we learn from patients into more effective treatments for childhood and rare cancers.”

title: Special Lecture Series: The NCI Childhood Cancer Data Initiative (CCDI)—Learning from Every Childhood Cancer Patient
body:
During this lecture, NCI volunteer staff member and Duke University’s Dr. Warren Kibbe will present on the NCI CCDI and will be specifically discussing: the current state of the initiative, where the project is headed, and how to get involved.

The NCI CCDI was announced by the White House and received its first allocation of funding in FY2020, with the goal of learning from every child and young adult with cancer to improve pediatric cancer treatment and data sharing integrity. hosted by Big Data Training for Cancer Research, a program of Purdue University’s Center for Cancer Research.

Warren Kibbe, Ph.D.

Dr. Kibbe is vice chair and professor for the Department of Biostatistics and Bioinformatics at Duke University and chief data officer at Duke Cancer Institute. He is also a volunteer staff member for NCI.

title: NCI Childhood Cancer Data Initiative Annual Symposium 2021
body:
Registration is now open for NCI’s Childhood Cancer Data Initiative (CCDI) Annual Symposium. Keep the momentum of the initiative going; attend to exchange information with other cancer researchers and learn about current cancer data activities. Email the CCDI if you have questions.

A full agenda for the event is available and includes NCI and community speakers. Below includes a truncated agenda with NCI staff presenters only: 11:00 a.m.– Welcome and NCI Speaker: Ned 11:15 CCDI Video Sharpless, M.D. a.m. 11:15 Keynote: a.m.– 11:30 Perspectives a.m. a.m.– and Current Mariotto, Ph.D.; Mary 12:50 Activities Frances Wedekind, M.D. p.m. 12:50 Opportunities for p.m.– NCI Speaker: Jim CCDI in the 1:00 Doroshow, M.D.

Coming Years p.m. 2:00 CCDI NCI Speakers: Betsy Hsu, p.m.– Opportunities in Ph.D.; Troy McEachron, 4:55 Three Focus Ph.D.; Jack Shern, M.D.; p.m. Areas Brigitte Widemann, M.D. 4:55 p.m.– Closing Remarks 5:00 p.m.

CCDI is building a community centered around childhood cancer and research data. Through enhanced data sharing, CCDI aims to improve our understanding of cancer biology to improve preventive measures, treatment, quality of life, and survivorship, as well as ensure that researchers learn from every child with cancer. Learn more at the CCDI webpage.

title: Childhood Cancer Data Initiative Webinar: Current Programs and Progress
body:
The Childhood Cancer Data Initiative (CCDI) Engagement Committee will host this webinar to provide an overview of the research initiative. This includes opening remarks from NCI Director, Dr. Ned Sharpless, about the initiative’s foundational goals, importance, and vision. This will also include updates from these speakers:

Dr. Lynn Penberthy, who will discuss the National Childhood Cancer Registry, which is being developed to leverage existing (and primarily adult) cancer registries to identify and follow childhood cancer cases in the U.S.

Dr. Jack Shern, who will talk about the Molecular Characterization Protocol, which is a strategy being developed to provide detailed clinical and molecular information to every child with cancer. data sharing to improve the understanding of cancer biology.

Deeper understanding of cancer biology will help improve cancer prevention measures, treatment, and quality of life for patients around the world.

Norman "Ned" Sharpless,

M.D.

Dr. Sharpless is the director of NCI.

Lynne Penberthy, M.D.,

MPH Dr. Penberthy is the associate director for the Surveillance Research Program, Division of Cancer Control and Population Sciences, NCI.

Jack Shern, M.D.

Dr. Shern is a physician scientist in the Pediatric Oncology Branch, Center for Cancer Research, NCI.

title: Watch the New Childhood Cancer Data Initiative Vision Video
body:
The NCI’s Childhood (CCDI) has released a new video about the initiative’s vision of building a community around childhood cancer and research data. Featured in the video are advocates such as Amanda Haddock, president and co-founder of Dragon Master Foundation, and pediatric cancer researchers like Dr. Ned Sharpless, NCI Director, and Dr. Sam Volchenboum, Director of the Pediatric Cancer Data aspirations to improve outcomes for treating children and adolescents and young adults (AYAs) with cancer.

CCDI’s goal is to maximize every opportunity to improve treatments and outcomes for children and AYAs with cancer.

To increase data use and sharing in pursuit of progress against childhood and AYA cancers, the CCDI aims to:

Establish a connected data infrastructure of repositories, registries, and tools to enable sharing of childhood and AYA cancer data from multiple sources (e.g., molecular, preclinical, biological, imaging, clinical trial data) Create meaningful data sets that have the proper information about each patient to generate novel discoveries as well as comprehensively address key questions in childhood and AYA cancers Identify opportunities to make data work better for patients, clinicians, and researchers Develop and enhance tools and methods to extract knowledge from data so different types of questions can be adequately addressed The CCDI is a federal investment of $50 million that was initiated in December 2019 and proposed to be extended in equal amounts per year for the next 9 years. These funds will allow NCI to enhance data sharing, collection, analysis, and access for ongoing and planned childhood and AYA cancer and survivorship research throughout the Institute. NCI Office of Data Sharing Director Dr. Jaime Guidry Auvil shared, “Our goal in optimizing how we collect, use, and share data is to truly learn all we can from every child so that every child can maximally benefit from what we learn.” Watch the new vision video and learn more about the CCDI.
